---
canvas:
  allowed_extensions:
  - pdf
  grading_type: pass_fail
  group_assignment: true
  group_set: Project Groups
  points: 1
  published: true
  submission_types:
  - online_upload
  type: assignment
title: Lab 9 – H-Bridge with MOSFETs and ESP32
---

## Learning Goals

- Build a complete MOSFET-based H-bridge controlled by the ESP32
- Combine concepts from Labs 6, 7, and 8 into a bi-directional motor speed controller
- Understand dead-time and why dedicated motor driver ICs exist

## Background

Replace the manual switches from Lab 8 with MOSFETs, using the circuit from the [H-Bridge Theory](01_HBridge_Theory.qmd) page:

- **High-side switches:** IRF9540/IRF9Z34 (P-channel) with 2N7000 level shifters (as in Lab 7)
- **Low-side switches:** IRL540N (N-channel, logic-level) driven directly by ESP32 (as in Lab 6)

Each high-side MOSFET needs:

- A 2N7000 level shifter with its gate connected to ESP32 GPIO
- A 10kΩ pull-up from the IRF9540/IRF9Z34 gate to $V_+$

Each low-side MOSFET needs:

- Gate connected directly to ESP32 GPIO
- A 10kΩ pull-down from gate to GND

## Components

- ESP32 DevKit
- 2× IRF9540/IRF9Z34 (P-channel MOSFET)
- 2× IRL540N (N-channel MOSFET, logic-level)
- 2× 2N7000 (level shifter)
- 4× 10kΩ resistors (2 pull-ups for high-side, 2 pull-downs for low-side)
- 1× Potentiometer (10kΩ)
- 1× Small DC motor
- 12V power supply with current limit

## Tasks

1. Build the MOSFET H-bridge on the breadboard. Use four ESP32 GPIO pins (e.g. GPIO 25, 26, 27, 32) — one per MOSFET/level-shifter pair.

::: {.callout-warning}
Tripple-check your connections! Use the simulator first!
:::

2. Write code to control direction and speed using a potentiometer:
   - Potentiometer center (~2048) = motor stopped
   - Turned right (>2048) = forward, speed proportional to position
   - Turned left (<2048) = reverse, speed proportional to position

3. **Important:** Implement dead-time (at least 10μs) when switching direction. Turn the active pair OFF, delay, then turn the new pair ON.


::: {.callout-warning}
Study the code below carefully! Do it in your group and explain every function to each other! Do not just copy-paste the code!!
:::

```cpp
#include <Arduino.h>

const int highLeftPin = 25;   // IRF9540/IRF9Z34 left (via 2N7000)
const int lowLeftPin = 26;    // IRL540N left   — PWM
const int highRightPin = 27;  // IRF9540/IRF9Z34 right (via 2N7000)
const int lowRightPin = 32;   // IRL540N right  — PWM
const int potPin = 34;

// LEDC PWM channels (one per low-side MOSFET)
const int lowLeftChannel  = 0;
const int lowRightChannel = 1;
const int pwmFreq = 20000;    // 20 kHz — above audible range
const int pwmResolution = 8;  // 8-bit: 0–255

const int deadTimeUs = 50;  // Dead-time between direction changes

void allOff() {
  digitalWrite(highLeftPin, LOW);
  digitalWrite(highRightPin, LOW);
  ledcWrite(lowLeftChannel, 0);
  ledcWrite(lowRightChannel, 0);
  delayMicroseconds(deadTimeUs);  // Guarantee dead-time on every transition
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(highLeftPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(highRightPin, OUTPUT);

  // Set up PWM on the two low-side gates
  ledcSetup(lowLeftChannel, pwmFreq, pwmResolution);
  ledcSetup(lowRightChannel, pwmFreq, pwmResolution);
  ledcAttachPin(lowLeftPin, lowLeftChannel);
  ledcAttachPin(lowRightPin, lowRightChannel);

  allOff();
}

void forward(int speed) {
  allOff();                               // includes dead-time
  digitalWrite(highLeftPin, HIGH);        // Left high ON
  ledcWrite(lowRightChannel, speed);      // Right low PWM
}

void reverse(int speed) {
  allOff();                               // includes dead-time
  digitalWrite(highRightPin, HIGH);       // Right high ON
  ledcWrite(lowLeftChannel, speed);       // Left low PWM
}

// Pot center and deadband (symmetric around the resting position)
const int potCenter   = 2048;
const int potDeadband = 100;   // ±100 counts → ignore noise/jitter near center

void loop() {
  int pot = analogRead(potPin);

  if (pot > potCenter + potDeadband) {
    int speed = map(pot, potCenter + potDeadband, 4095, 0, 255);
    forward(speed);
  } else if (pot < potCenter - potDeadband) {
    int speed = map(pot, potCenter - potDeadband, 0, 0, 255);
    reverse(speed);
  } else {
    allOff();  // Inside deadband — motor stopped
  }
  delay(20);
}
```

4. Test forward and reverse. Observe the motor direction changing smoothly.
5. **Measure** with the oscilloscope: capture the gate signals during a direction change. Is the dead-time visible?
6. Count the total number of components in your H-bridge (MOSFETs, resistors, level shifters). Compare with the BTS7960 module (next lab).

## Questions

1. How many discrete components does your H-bridge require? How does this compare to using a BTS7960?
2. What would happen without dead-time when switching direction rapidly?
3. Why do commercial motor drivers include features like over-current protection and thermal shutdown?
4. In the truth table, what is the difference between coast and brake? Which did you implement in `allOff()`?

## Submission

Write a short lab report in Quarto following the [Report Writing Guide](../01_Fundamentals/01_Report_Writing_Guide.qmd). Include:

- A **circuit diagram** (hand-drawn or schematic) showing all components with labels
- Your code with comments explaining the dead-time implementation
- Oscilloscope screenshots showing the gate signals
- A component count comparison between your discrete H-bridge and the BTS7960
- Answers to all questions

Render to PDF and upload.
